paragraphs.body: Can a woman do anything without being made fun of? I have thought about this question from different angles, but they all have the same answer. They can’t. When a girl is overly friendly with a guy, she is labeled a “pick me,” if a girl speaks up for herself, she is too “bitchy,” and when a girl likes a sport of any kind, the first question a man will bring up is “name three players?” Society has made its mark on women by never allowing them to be enough. We must be everything and nothing at the same time. If boys will be boys, girls can be girls too.

As a cynosure of motorsports, I have always been curious about the women that have dared to enter such a masculine-presenting sport. I never seem to hear about these courageous women putting themselves in the shoes of most men. I have never even heard of the name Danica Patrick before having to research women in motorsports. Now that I have read of her, she is just too iconic. In an interview with The Spun, Danica said she “didn’t feel welcome in England as a girl” (Koons). Furthermore, according to femalesinmotorsport.com, she is the only woman to win an Indy series in 2008 (Thomson). Basically, throughout her career, she faced the struggles of discrimination against her gender, but despite her trials and tribulations, she let her skills do all the talking. Standing at the top of the podium with two men below her and all the people who doubted her must have tasted so much better than receiving the trophy. Then again, as interesting as the actual driver may sound, what happens behind the scenes is just as important. You could call these people “the guy in the chair” or, in this case, “girl.” With the overwhelming popularity F1 has received over the years, Elle magazine points out that “we could see the arrival of more female mechanics, more women race engineers and even the next female Gunther, Christian or Toto” (Bird). All of which are jobs dominated by men. It appears Women are always a step behind. When will society give women a seat next to men?

It is no surprise that Women and Men are not equal in the eyes of society. There will always be a matter of who is better at what. Not to mention the biological standpoints, that is for another essay. Everything that a man can be is what a woman can be. I don’t understand why that is so hard for some people to grasp. We are not asking to be treated like we are men, we are asking to be treated like a human. A human with complex feelings and ideas. A human who dreams of the impossible and challenges the world, like every other human being. I truly believe that women have so much power and determination. We were born into a world with stereotypes and judgment against people who are not white men. If we don’t stick to what we are good at and what is believed to be the norm, we are shunned and called out for being selfish. Humans saw the beautiful dynamics of nature and created a barrier against women. Future presidents, role models, mothers, daughters, Formula One drivers, and engineers. These are just a few examples of what women can achieve, and If I must be selfish and called a “bitch” then so be it. I will be the bitchist bitch if that means I can reach the same goals as any other man.

I can acknowledge the fact that motorsports are not a simple sport. You can put on a pair of cleats and play for a soccer team and be called a soccer player, but you can’t put on a helmet and get into a Formula 1 car and call yourself a driver. This is because Formula One drivers are high-performance athletes who test the limits of pure carbon fiber and push through gravity as if it did not affect them. It is just simply not for everyone. Meanwhile, this doesn’t mean it’s just for men. Whoever says it is is living in the 1800s. Both men and women have impressively made history in motorsports. The sport may use language that has masculine connotations, and it may have started with just men in the sport, but that’s because mostly everything was started by men. They had the resources, the money, and the support. Now that we live in a world where equality and human rights are a given, women are slowly entering the game. To start, F1 Academy, a one-make racing series, has opened its arms to 15 drivers. A grid solely dedicated to women in F1. It may not be a part of the big leagues, but societal changes cannot be made overnight. F1 Academy is essentially the new beginning for future women in motorsports and sports in general.

As a new fan of motorsports, I have had my share of comments from other men and women questioning me to see if I am qualified. I have been told that I only watch the sport for the physically attractive men. Although it may have started as an admiration of their handsomeness, I fell in love with the adrenaline it gives me every time I see a crash, an overtake, or a battle for a place on the podium. It’s like a game of chess. Strategy is the most important part of the race. The temperature of the tyres, the circuit layout, the engine of the car, and the aerodynamics all play a part. This is why I have so much admiration for the men and women in the sport. A driver must push the limits of the human body if they want to win. So, it’s not about who you are, your background, or your gender. It is about your skill, precision, and your mindset. That is why I love being a woman in motorsports.

annotations.comment: The photos are great! The photo of Danica with the trophy speaks directly to your central focusing ideas :) Other than the images, I don't see any changes to the essay, so the grade in the other areas mostly stays the same. But good work!
